claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit! Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to know about your child (or children). 

**First question: How old is your child?**

This is important because the age determines the basic eligibility:
- Children **under 16** automatically qualify (subject to other conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** are not eligible

Please tell me your child's age. If you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, we can go through them one at a time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I'm interested in finding out if I can claim Child Benefit. I've got three kids actually - Alex is 5, Blake is 17, and Charlie is 19. Can you help me figure out if I'm eligible for them?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Absolutely! I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for all three of your children: Alex (5), Blake (17), and Charlie (19). Let me work through each child's situation.

Let's start with **Alex (age 5)**:

Since Alex is under 16, they would generally qualify as a qualifying child. However, I need to check one important thing:

**Is Alex working full-time (defined as 24 hours or more per week)?**

(This is very unlikely at age 5, but I need to confirm this as part of the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - Alex is only 5, so definitely not working at all!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That makes sense. So **Alex qualifies** as a qualifying child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now let me continue checking Alex's eligibility:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

(Again, very unlikely at age 5, but I need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue the assessment for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program?**

(Again, not applicable at age 5, but let me confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any apprenticeship - far too young for that!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Now let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check **your residency status** (this applies to all three children):

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

If yes, that satisfies the residency requirement. 

If not, there are other ways you might qualify, such as:
- Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- Being a Crown servant living abroad
- Other special circumstances

Do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That satisfies the residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now let me check about your responsibility for the children. This question applies to all three of your children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie):

**Are you responsible for your children?**

You're considered responsible if ANY of the following apply:
- You **live with the child**, OR
- You **contribute to their upkeep** (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit toward their care - things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents), OR
- You **pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you for your children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they all live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That establishes responsibility for all three children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of your three children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie)?**

For example, is another parent, guardian, or family member claiming Child Benefit for any of them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check the living situation for each of your children. Let me ask about all three:

**Are any of your children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) currently:**
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?

Or are they all living normally at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're all living normally at home with me - none of them are in hospital, care, or being fostered.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
One final question:

**Is the local council paying towards any of your children's accommodation or maintenance?**

(You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the council is paying for the child's upkeep.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Now let me check the situation for your other two children - **Blake (17)** and **Charlie (19)**.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! So **Alex is eligible** for Child Benefit. Now let me assess Blake and Charlie, as their ages require additional checks.

Let me restart the assessment for **Blake (age 17)**:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For **Blake (age 17)**, I need to know:

**Is Blake currently in approved education or training, OR has Blake left education/training recently (within the last 20 weeks)?**

The options are:
- Currently in education or training
- Left education/training recently (within the last 20 weeks)
- Neither

Which applies to Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake isn't in education or training anymore, and it's been longer than 20 weeks since leaving.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Unfortunately, **Blake is not eligible** for Child Benefit because at age 17, Blake needs to be in approved education or training, or have left education within the last 20 weeks to qualify for the extension period. Since it's been longer than 20 weeks, Blake doesn't meet the criteria.

Now let me check **Charlie (age 19)**:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For **Charlie (age 19)**, the same rules apply as for Blake:

**Is Charlie currently in approved education or training, OR has Charlie left education/training recently (within the last 20 weeks)?**

Which applies to Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Charlie is currently in education - doing A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! A-levels are approved education. Now I need to check a few more details about Charlie's situation:

**Is Charlie's A-level course full-time** (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's a full-time A-level course.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! A-levels are approved education. Now let me continue checking Charlie's eligibility:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Charlie working less than 24 hours per week** (or not in paid work at all)?

Children aged 16+ who work 24 hours or more per week are not eligible for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie doesn't work at all - just focuses on studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **Is Charlie receiving any benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Charlie in any type of apprenticeship program?**

(You mentioned A-levels, so I'm assuming not, but I need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie is just doing A-levels, not an apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now since we've already established that you meet the residency requirements, you're responsible for Charlie, no one else is claiming, Charlie lives at home with you, and the council isn't paying for Charlie's upkeep, let me navigate through those steps for Charlie's assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Now let me provide you with the complete assessment for all three of your children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility**

Based on my assessment, here's the outcome for each of your children:

### ✅ **Alex (age 5) - ELIGIBLE**
Alex qualifies for Child Benefit as a child under 16. All eligibility criteria are met.

### ❌ **Blake (age 17) - NOT ELIGIBLE**
Unfortunately, Blake does not qualify because:
- Blake is not currently in approved education or training
- It's been more than 20 weeks since leaving education
- For 16-19 year olds, they must be in approved education/training OR have left within the last 20 weeks to qualify

### ✅ **Charlie (age 19) - ELIGIBLE**
Charlie qualifies for Child Benefit as a 16-19 year old in approved full-time education (A-levels).

---

## **Next Steps for Alex and Charlie:**

1. **Apply for Child Benefit:**
   - Apply online at: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim
   - Or by post using form CH2

2. **Documents you'll need:**
   - Proof of identity for yourself
   - Birth certificates for Alex and Charlie

3. **Important note about high earners:**
   - If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (which can reduce or eliminate the benefit)
   - You can still claim to protect National Insurance credits

4. **Payments:**
   - Paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

---

## **Regarding Blake:**

If Blake returns to approved education or training in the future, you could become eligible for Child Benefit for Blake at that time. Approved options include A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQ Level 3 or below, and similar non-advanced courses (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study).

Do you have any questions about this assessment?

reasonings: Alex (age 5) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. As a child under 16 who is not working full-time, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, living with you in the UK, with no other claimant, and not supported by the local council, Alex meets all the qualifying child criteria.

Blake (age 17) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. While Blake lives with you and you meet all the claimant requirements, Blake does not meet the qualifying child criteria for someone aged 16-19. Blake is not currently in approved education or training, and it has been more than 20 weeks since leaving education. For children aged 16-19, they must either be in approved education/training or have left within the last 20 weeks to qualify for the extension period.

Charlie (age 19) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Charlie is in full-time approved education (A-levels, which are non-advanced qualifications), is not working 24+ hours per week, is not receiving benefits in their own right, is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, lives with you in the UK, with no other claimant, and is not supported by the local council. Charlie meets all the qualifying child criteria for someone aged 16-19 in approved education.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for 2 out of your 3 children: Alex (age 5) and Charlie (age 19). Alex qualifies as a child under 16, and Charlie qualifies as a 16-19 year old in approved full-time education (A-levels). Blake (age 17) does not qualify because Blake is not in education or training and it has been more than 20 weeks since leaving education. You meet all the claimant requirements including UK residency, responsibility for the children, and no competing claims.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 5) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. As a child under 16 who is not working full-time, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, living with you in the UK, with no other claimant, and not supported by the local council, Alex meets all the qualifying child criteria.

Blake (age 17) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. While Blake lives with you and you meet all the claimant requirements, Blake does not meet the qualifying child criteria for someone aged 16-19. Blake is not currently in approved education or training, and it has been more than 20 weeks since leaving education. For children aged 16-19, they must either be in approved education/training or have left within the last 20 weeks to qualify for the extension period.

Charlie (age 19) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Charlie is in full-time approved education (A-levels, which are non-advanced qualifications), is not working 24+ hours per week, is not receiving benefits in their own right, is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, lives with you in the UK, with no other claimant, and is not supported by the local council. Charlie meets all the qualifying child criteria for someone aged 16-19 in approved education.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for 2 out of your 3 children: Alex (age 5) and Charlie (age 19). Alex qualifies as a child under 16, and Charlie qualifies as a 16-19 year old in approved full-time education (A-levels). Blake (age 17) does not qualify because Blake is not in education or training and it has been more than 20 weeks since leaving education. You meet all the claimant requirements including UK residency, responsibility for the children, and no competing claims.
